text: Hello friends. This is a case of laparoscopic myomectomy. This patient is a forty-five year old female. Post-menopausal she started again bleeding and DUB. And there was a deep intramural myoma with submucous extension. So one third of the myoma is in the cavity and two third is in the muscle.

So we will do the laparoscopic myomectomy. And there is a fair chance that the cavity will open during laparoscopic myomectomy. This is only six centimeter myoma, but deeply situated. So this is the target.
The surgeon positions the supraumbilical port for the laparoscopic camera, using the thumb to guide the telescope while the index finger serves as a target for precise placement.

The surgeon marks the surgical site using an Induco Karamine Sketch Pen, which is an anti-infectant surgical marker. The right hand port is positioned, and the left hand port is placed 7.5 centimeters lateral and below.
A small stab wound is made supraumbilically for the insertion of a Veress needle, mirroring the position of the left-hand port.

The surgeon introduces the Veress needle into the abdomen, lifting the abdominal wall to ensure perpendicular entry. After hearing two clicks, the needle enters the abdominal cavity. The irrigation and suction tests are performed, followed by the hanging drop test, where the drop is sucked into the cavity. Pneumoperitoneum is established with approximately three liters of gas to reach the preset pressure of fifteen mmHg, with an initial flow rate of one liter per minute.

After creating pneumoperitoneum, the surgeon removes the varus needle and enlarges the incision to 11 mm. Using a double-hand technique, the trocar is introduced, followed by the telescope for initial diagnostic laparoscopy. The laparoscopic view reveals the bowel positioned with the head at a 30-degree downward angle, and a deep intramural myoma located on the posterior uterine wall.

The surgeon injects vasopressin diluted with approximately 200 ml of saline into the uterus, creating transient ischemia and hydro dissection. The uterus exhibits a marble white appearance due to the vasopressin effect. A myoma screw is applied to the uterus.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to make an elliptical incision on the uterus. An assistant helps by applying a myoma screw to stabilize the deep intramural myoma. The incision is then enlarged from the opposite side.

The surgeon uses a myoma screw in the left hand to stabilize the deep intramural myoma while enucleating it. Another myoma screw is positioned on the right side. The muscle is carefully cut using suction irrigation, with the harmonic device aiding in separation and dissection.

The surgeon uses suction irrigation to enucleate a six-centimeter myoma from the uterine cavity. The myoma, which was deeply embedded with fifty percent in the uterine lumen, is now fully separated and visible outside the cavity. The uterine cavity is open, and the next step will involve closing the incision.
The surgeon uses a harmonic device to enucleate and remove additional myomas, including a left cornual myoma, which is visibly separated and extracted from the uterine tissue.

The surgeon begins suturing the uterine wall using extracorporeal knots to close the filling defect. The knots are tied and pulled, visibly opposing the wall without any bleeding. The Western knot technique is employed to ensure secure closure.

The surgeon applies two to three extracorporeal western knots to approximate the large gap in the tissue. The knots act as tension sutures, effectively closing the defect. A dandy jamming knot is used initially, followed by continuous suturing of the deep muscular layer, resembling a baseball stitch, to ensure proper approximation of the layers.

The surgeon uses the left hand to hold the suture while the right hand takes bites with a number one white grill suture and round body needle, interlocking the sutures to approximate the margins of the incision. The suturing is performed quickly to prevent bleeding, aided by the vasopressin effect.

The surgeon performs deep muscular continuous suturing to approximate the margins of the uterine tissue. An assistant holds the tail of an extracorporeal western knot, while the surgeon interlocks the suture to prevent loosening. The muscle flap is pushed inside, and the suture is placed over it to close a small tear, ensuring the tissue is tightly secured.

The surgeon performs tight suturing on the deep myoma using extracorporeal knots, avoiding barb sutures due to tension concerns. The suturing progresses to a healthy area to prevent cutting through, ensuring the strength of the uterus with a double layer technique.

The surgeon performs a multi-layer closure using various knot techniques. A Skinner surgeon's knot is applied for the open cavity, followed by a tumble square knot for the muscular layer. An extracorporeal square or Misrash knot is used to oppose tension, and a Dundee jamming knot is applied for continuous suturing. The serous layer is closed with an Everdeen termination. The suture is tightly closed, and the same suture is used to return back, terminating with a long tail of the extracorporeal knot.

The surgeon completes the suturing with a double wrap followed by two alternating wraps. Minimal blood loss is observed, and suction irrigation is performed. A 15mm morcellator is used to morcellate the tissue, followed by port closure using a cobbler's needle to push and pull the suture through the port site.
So thank you very much for watching this video. Have a nice day.
